Choosing the right WordPress developer is one of the most important decisions you will make when building or improving your business website.
A visually attractive website is not enough. It should also be fast, responsive, secure, easy to manage, search-engine friendly and designed around your business goals. The developer you hire can influence website performance, user experience, online sales and long-term maintenance.
WordPress offers flexible themes, plugins and publishing tools, but creating a professional business website still requires careful planning and technically sound implementation. You can review the
official WordPress features
to understand the platform’s core capabilities.
Quick Answer: How Do You Choose a WordPress Developer?
Choose a developer who understands your business objectives, has relevant WordPress experience, can show genuine project work and follows a clear development process.
Review relevant WordPress projects and live websites.
Check mobile responsiveness and performance knowledge.
Confirm the project scope, timeline and revision process.
Ask how security, backups and SEO will be handled.
Confirm website ownership and post-launch support.
What Does a WordPress Developer Do?
A WordPress developer builds, customizes, improves and maintains websites using WordPress. Depending on the project, the developer may handle website architecture, theme customization, Elementor development, WooCommerce setup, responsive design, plugin configuration, speed optimization, migration, security and technical SEO.
Explore my
WordPress website development services
to see the types of business websites and technical solutions available.
1. Define Your Website Goals Before Hiring
Before contacting a developer, identify what the website should accomplish. A developer cannot recommend the right solution without understanding the purpose of the project.
Do you need a new website or a redesign?
Is your goal to generate leads, sell products or accept bookings?
Who is your target audience?
Which pages, forms and integrations do you need?
Do you already have branding, content and images?
What are your expected timeline and budget range?
A professional developer should ask about your business and customers before recommending a theme, builder or plugin.
2. Look for Relevant WordPress Experience
General web-development knowledge is valuable, but it does not always prove strong WordPress expertise. A qualified developer should understand:
WordPress installation and configuration
Themes, child themes and templates
Elementor or the WordPress block editor
Responsive layouts
Forms and email delivery
Plugins and compatibility
Security, backups and updates
Performance optimization
HTML, CSS, JavaScript and basic PHP concepts
Learn more about my experience, skills and working approach on the
About Akaeid Hasan
page.
3. Review the Developer’s Portfolio Carefully
A portfolio should demonstrate more than attractive screenshots. Look at how each website performs as a complete business tool.
Desktop and mobile design quality
Navigation and content structure
Page-loading experience
Forms and conversion elements
Product or service presentation
Consistency across different pages
Real live website URLs where available
Review my
WordPress website portfolio
for examples of business, ecommerce, portfolio and content-focused websites.
4. Understand Website Design and Development
Website design and development are connected, but they solve different parts of the project.
Website design focuses on:
Layout, colour, typography, visual hierarchy, branding, user journey and calls to action.
Website development focuses on:
WordPress configuration, functionality, responsive behaviour, forms, integrations, performance and security.
For a complete visual and user-experience solution, review my
WordPress website design service.
5. Check Mobile Responsiveness and Accessibility
Your website should be usable on desktop computers, tablets and smartphones. Mobile responsiveness involves more than shrinking a desktop layout.
Readable font sizes
Touch-friendly buttons
Simple mobile navigation
Responsive images and tables
Easy-to-complete forms
Correct spacing and content order
Accessibility should also be considered during development. The
W3C accessibility introduction
explains why websites should support users with different abilities, devices and browsing conditions.
6. Evaluate Website Speed Knowledge
A slow website can frustrate visitors and reduce enquiries or sales. A skilled developer should understand common performance problems such as:
Oversized images
Too many plugins
Unoptimized fonts
Heavy page-builder elements
Poor caching
Slow hosting
Unnecessary third-party scripts
Google provides
Core Web Vitals guidance
for evaluating loading performance, responsiveness and visual stability.
7. Confirm Basic SEO Knowledge
A website developer does not always replace a complete SEO specialist, but the website should be built on a search-friendly technical foundation.
Logical heading structure
Clean and readable URLs
SEO titles and meta descriptions
Image alt text
Contextual internal links
XML sitemap and robots settings
Canonical URLs and redirects
Schema markup and indexability
Google recommends creating useful and people-first content instead of content designed mainly to manipulate rankings. Read the
official helpful-content guidance.
For page titles, headings, content structure, internal linking and image optimization, see my
WordPress on-page SEO service.
8. Choose the Right Developer for WooCommerce
An ecommerce website requires more planning than a basic informational website. A WooCommerce developer may need to configure:
Products and variations
Inventory and categories
Payments, taxes and shipping
Checkout and customer accounts
Order emails and coupons
Analytics and store security
The
official WooCommerce documentation
covers the connected areas involved in managing an online store.
For online-store development, explore my
WooCommerce ecommerce website service.
9. Check Figma-to-WordPress Experience
When a website design already exists in Figma, the developer must convert that design into a responsive, functional and manageable WordPress website.
Review the Figma design and reusable components.
Plan desktop, tablet and mobile behaviour.
Build the layouts in WordPress.
Optimize fonts and images.
Configure forms and interactions.
Test responsiveness and browser compatibility.
Complete performance and quality checks.
Learn about my
Figma to WordPress development service.
10. Decide Whether You Need a Redesign
You may not need to replace your entire website. A redesign may be appropriate when the existing website has:
An outdated appearance
Poor mobile usability
Confusing navigation
Slow loading
Weak calls to action
Broken functionality
Poor conversion performance
Explore my
WordPress website redesign service
to understand how an existing website can be improved without rebuilding everything unnecessarily.
11. Ask About the Development Process
A clear process reduces delays, unexpected costs and communication problems.
Discovery: Understand the business, audience, goals and functionality.
Planning: Confirm the sitemap, features and project scope.
Design: Prepare or approve the page layouts.
Development: Build the website and configure the required features.
Testing: Test responsiveness, forms, links and performance.
Launch: Publish the website and complete final checks.
Support: Resolve agreed post-launch issues and provide guidance.
12. Clarify Pricing, Scope and Ownership
A professional proposal should clearly explain:
Number of pages and features
Design and content responsibilities
Plugin and licence costs
Revisions and project timeline
Hosting, maintenance and support
Payment schedule and exclusions
You should also confirm who will own the domain, hosting account, WordPress access, analytics accounts, backups and premium licences after the project is completed.
Questions to Ask Before Hiring
Can you show relevant WordPress projects?
Which theme, builder or development approach will you use?
Will the website be easy for me to update?
How will mobile responsiveness be tested?
What performance optimization is included?
Will you implement basic on-page SEO?
How will backups and security be handled?
How many revisions are included?
What is the estimated timeline?
What post-launch support is included?
Freelance WordPress Developer vs Agency
Factor Freelance Developer WordPress Agency
Communication Direct with the developer Often through a manager
Pricing Usually more flexible Usually higher overhead
Project Size Small and medium projects Large multidisciplinary projects
Relationship Personal collaboration Agency-managed process
Warning Signs to Avoid
Guaranteeing a number-one Google ranking
Being unable to show relevant work
Using copied portfolio screenshots
Recommending many plugins without explanation
Avoiding discussions about performance or security
Providing no written project scope
Refusing to provide administrative access
Offering no testing or post-launch support

Frequently Asked Questions
How much does it cost to hire a WordPress developer?
The cost depends on page count, design complexity, ecommerce functionality, integrations, content requirements and project timeline.
How long does WordPress development take?
A small business website may require several days or weeks. Ecommerce, membership and custom websites usually require more time.
Can a developer improve my existing website?
Yes. A developer can redesign the layout, improve mobile usability, optimize speed, fix functionality and strengthen the technical SEO foundation.
Can a WordPress developer build an online store?
A developer with WooCommerce experience can configure products, checkout, payments, shipping, customer accounts and order-management features.
Will I be able to update the website myself?
A properly developed WordPress website should allow you to update text, images, products and basic page content without rebuilding the website.
Final Thoughts
Learning how to choose a WordPress developer can help you avoid delays, technical problems and unnecessary redevelopment costs.
Look beyond attractive designs and low prices. Evaluate the developer’s experience, portfolio, technical knowledge, communication, project process, testing standards and post-launch support.
